text: The robotic-assisted laparoscopic prostatectomy is performed using the da Vinci SI system, which has four arms. The left and right robotic arms are visible, with the left arm equipped with a bipolar instrument used for grasping.

The robotic system's right arm uses scissors to perform dissection while the assistant tentatively elevates the peritoneum, providing counter-traction against the bipolar grasping instrument in the left arm.
The surgeon performs a robotic prostatectomy using a transperitoneal approach, accessing the prostate by incising through the peritoneum. The posterior approach is utilized during this procedure.
The surgeon uses the posterior approach to cut the vas deferens and dissect the seminal vesicles, providing a clear view of these structures before proceeding to drop the bladder.

Using a transperitoneal approach has some theoretical downsides because where you are in the peritoneum, you're worried about contaminating the peritoneum and the extraperitoneum, meaning, for example, you could have a urine leak that could come into the peritoneal space. With the robot and with experience, this is very rare, we make sure we have a watertight anastomosis between the bladder and urethra prior to finishing the case.

The surgeon performs dissection to free the vas deferens, proceeding to locate the right and left seminal vesicles. A bipolar instrument set at thirty is used to minimize monopolar cautery, ensuring precision and reducing current travel near nerve and vascular tissue surrounding the seminal vesicles. Blunt and non-cautery dissection is employed as the surgeon approaches the tips of the seminal vesicles.

If you're learning how to do this case, it's always important to think when you're not proceeding with the case what your assistant could be doing to help you. Your assistant isn't a robot, and sometimes they have a mind of their own, and it's fine if you guys realize that things aren't progressing, take a step back and also ask yourself what could the assistant be doing. We have... an assistant who's been with our team for a long time and it's second nature for him and myself when we do these cases, but it's doable as long as you're persisting with kind of always thinking when the case is not moving, what could we be doing to make things better?

The surgeon dissects through Denonvilliers' fascia, lifting both seminal vesicles to enhance exposure. The assistant uses cold water irrigation to maintain a clear surgical field, which also aids in cooling the tissue during cautery use. The dissection progresses towards the apex, creating a working space for subsequent steps.

The surgeon uses a grasping instrument to pull on the right or left medial umbilical ligament, employing sharp and blunt dissection techniques to open the extraperitoneal space. A visible line of dissection appears as traction is applied, aiding in the efficient exposure of the extraperitoneal space.

The surgeon exposes the endopelvic fascia and identifies the pubic symphysis, with the border of resection extending to the vas deferens. The prostate is visible below the bipolar instrument, and the dissection proceeds towards the superficial dorsal vein and puboprostatic ligaments on the right side of the prostate. The surgeon then moves back towards the camera port to locate the uracus, noting that the bladder may extend high up in some cases.

The surgeon quickly mirrors the dissection steps on the left side, having already established the anatomical landmarks such as the vas deferens, endopelvic fascia, and pubic symphysis on the right side.

The surgeon begins defatting the prostate, revealing minimal fat overlying the prostate tissue. The right endopelvic fascia is opened, exposing the levator musculature adjacent to the scissors. Cold irrigation is used to maintain tissue temperature, while bipolar cautery is available to minimize heat spread during dissection.

The surgeon begins the bladder dissection, carefully preserving surrounding tissue near the dorsal vein complex to protect the adjacent neurovascular bundle. The dorsal vein complex has not yet been taken, and the surgeon is prepared to manage any bleeding that may occur.

The surgeon uses the fourth arm of the robotic system to hold the Foley catheter up against the sidewall of the pelvis, exposing the bladder neck area for dissection. The posterior bladder neck dissection is underway, with careful attention to maintaining the proper thickness of the bladder posteriorly.

The surgeon encounters a thin posterior bladder wall, requiring careful navigation to avoid the ureteral orifices and trigone area. Watery bleeding indicates progress in connecting surgical areas. Adjustments are made to reduce bleeding near the prostate, revealing the vas deferens and seminal vesicles. The assistant uses suction to maintain traction on the posterior bladder wall, aiding in the exposure of these structures.

The surgeon uses two graspers to manage unexpected bleeding while proceeding with nerve-sparing techniques. The approach combines retrograde and anti-grade methods, aiming to preserve vascular tissue by staying higher than traditional techniques. The surgeon carefully identifies the plane for nerve sparing, adjusting angles to avoid entering the prostate while maintaining effective nerve preservation.

The surgeon begins the anti-grade nerve sparing technique, focusing on securing the left vascular pedicle of the prostate. The surgical field shows the space created earlier in the procedure, which is now being expanded. Large hemlock clips are applied to secure the pedicle, and clips are used to prevent back bleeding. The surgeon carefully isolates the vascular pedicle while maintaining a position above the neurovascular bundle, occasionally using bipolar cautery if necessary.

The surgeon has navigated through the vascular pedicle and is now positioned to perform nerve sparing near the apex of the prostate. There are visible attachments close to the apex, where minor bleeding occurs. The surgeon uses pinpoint cautery while the assistant irrigates the area, managing the minimal bleeding. The neuromuscular bundle on the left side appears healthy, and the surgeon makes cuts to approach the prostate apex, considering options for securing the dorsal vein complex.

The surgeon moves to the right side of the prostate, utilizing the fourth robotic arm for assistance. The pedicle is secured, and the prostate is freed on the left side, allowing more freedom of movement for the robotic instruments. The surgeon begins conventional nerve sparing in a retrograde fashion, establishing a plane for nerve sparing while reducing tissue tension. The tissue requires more effort to free, potentially due to inflammation from previous biopsies or prostatitis.

The surgeon uses a CT-1 needle with 2-0 PDS suture to secure the dorsal vein, tying a slip knot to minimize bleeding. After placing the stitch, another stitch is anchored to the pubic symphysis to replicate the urethrovesical angle post-prostate removal.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection at the apex of the prostate, carefully pinching and cutting through the dorsal vein complex to preserve the urethral sphincter and maximize urethral length. Pinpoint bipolar cautery is applied around the apex to eliminate any residual prostatic cells. The neurovascular bundles are visible, merging together underneath the prostate, highlighting the vascular nature of the tissue.

The surgeon applies FloSeal to achieve hemostasis in the surgical field, addressing minimal bleeding. Pressure is applied to enhance the hemostatic effect of FloSeal. The procedure then resumes with the urethrovesical anastomosis using a monocryl suture, which is preferred for its ability to seal gaps effectively.

The surgeon irrigates the surgical field, revealing clumps of FloSeal hemostatic agent. As the bladder is elevated, it aids in tamponade of the sinuses. On the left side, a clear suture is used, while a colored suture is applied on the opposite side to avoid confusion during the procedure.

The surgeon prepares to apply an amniotic wrap around the surgical site before bringing the bladder to the urethra. The wrap, made from amniotic tissue, is intended to prevent scarring in nerve-sparing cases.

One of the tips that we give other surgeons who are trying to perform the case is with the performance of the anastomosis, it's very important to keep both of your instruments in view. The suture can easily break if you don't do this. The other tip is to try to only have one hand on the suture at a time. If you have both hands on the suture, then the chances increase that you're applying undue or unrecognized tension, which will snap the suture, and then have to potentially have to have you start this all over again.

The surgeon tests the completed anastomosis by filling the bladder with 120 cc of fluid to check for leaks. Monocryl sutures are used to eliminate any gaps, and additional sutures are placed if necessary. The pneumoperitoneum is adjusted to assess bleeding, which is minimal, before concluding the procedure and placing a drain.
